messages.message: https://x.com/thevatstack/status/2075454206333591998 boost if you like it pls

I'll keep thinking about this because the Elo has always seemed fundamental to me

Two theologians claim to have the answer, they seem to have conflicting answers. We have one ship to steer. Who do we go with?

I think there's two ways to make that decision

- listen to both and decide (track record doesn't matter, we do our best to understand the situation and both their inputs)
- defer to established hierarchy (which we're already doing by deferring to _the theologians_ as a category)
I think the other fundamental reason is that this is meant to become the standard default social media algorithm

- who do you learn from, ups their elo
- who learns from you, ups your elo

what you teach may be completely false trash but if a million people learn from you daily, that gives you elo X

and then if you learn from Tom, now Tom has a very high elo because he's your source.
(maybe what I am measuring is degree of influence over minds)

-------

- If I wake up and see this long message from Defender, but it's noise (U) then his elo ranking should go down (relative to me)
- but instead of this making someone go down "globally", it does down relative to me
- I want a mechanism where people put information in and it rises like a hot air balloon
This comment on substack, I should be able to click on this guy and be able to see everyone he holds in high regard as sources of good & true information

I think making a list of important people you follow and what you trust them on, and maintaining that, is a lot of work. 

it can be generated from you just marking things A/B/U

it's getting hierarchical

https://x.com/Esychology/status/2075259224255349234

-------

I think I made a breakthrough with the "wipe the server every week" thing. I think I only need that for the "whiteboard", which is a derivative of the content here

I think we do absolutely want 

1. complete archives and ability to cross link etc 
2. and my ability to draw maps of the current knowledge base, and maintain a "whiteboard" that gets wiped clean every week

Ha — that's my screwup, and it's a fun one to explain: I gave the CI commits the email zero-bot@users.noreply.github.com, and that email namespace maps to real GitHub accounts by username. Someone on GitHub actually owns the name zero-bot, so GitHub attributed our robot's commits to that random person and listed him as a contributor. Fixing it properly — switch to the official Actions bot identity and rewrite the mis-attributed commit:

I think it's the account that pushes the change...?

Thinking about this one. Probably going to ramble a bit.

Worst is heavily subjective here so picking between an individual or group can change this response significantly. There's also a theory of mind failure mode here where you might not know the worst thing you did. Guess a layer deeper there's knowledge issues where something bad might not even be on your radar. Values change over time and we don't have a good way of handling that it seems.
We can look at some ancient culture and generally make an exception, but something bad from a decade ago tends to taint it.
So, I think the opening is "here is the worst thing I have done" but there's a closing in "and here is what I've done about it".

Rupture/repair cycle. I think in general repair feels more culturally weak and that's probably why we see so many things falling apart. It takes continuous effort to keep something alive and not decay.
Perhaps people become distrustful of repair because it's more costly to be wrong and there's a lot of repair manipulation.
Dunbar number small, population of online transpacialized people far exceeds it so it's cheaper to cut people out forever. Blocked bye! 💅

The Platner case is interesting since people were forced to make some judgement here. Pick a guy with seemingly known skeletons in the closet or rolling the dice on no apparent skeletons. 

Side note it's funny that the longer someone is in politics it's assumed the closet is full of skeletons even if we don't see them.

I'd prefer politicians to have a ton of pressure on them and see their closet skeletons, but crowd wise it seems preferable to have it not in sight. That's been one of the more interesting bits about Trump in that people are rabid to uncover anything bad which has made him probably the most vetted president ever whatever you think about where he stands.
How do you tell if a guy has no skeletons in the closet or he's really good at hiding them?
Then, there's also the meta game of revealing lesser skeletons you resolved to mask the larger unresolved ones.
https://www.blackcube.com/case-studies#Uncovering-Negative
